question: 4. The Euclidean plane is divided into regions by drawing a finite number of circles. Show that it is possible to color each of these regions either red or blue in such a way that no two adjacent regions have the same color. (Two such regions are said to be adjacent if and only if their boundaries have an arc of a circle in common.)
solution:
Solution. For each region into which the plane is divided, count the number of circles within which that region lies. If this number is odd color the region red, while if the number is even color it blue.

Then, if two regions share a common boundary arc, one of these regions is interior to one more of the given circles than is the other region. Hence these two regions have different colors.

Remark. A number of coloring problems (from the simple up to the four-color problem) are discussed in the pamphlet by E. B. Dynkin and V. A. Uspenskii, Multicolor Problems, trans. from the Russian, Heath, Boston, 1963.

kernel_variant.question:
Let $M^{n}$ be a closed, connected, orientable, smooth $n$-manifold with $n\ge 2$.  
A countable, locally-finite family  
\[
\mathcal H=\{\Sigma_{1},\Sigma_{2},\dots\}
\]
of pairwise distinct, connected, two-sided, smoothly embedded closed hypersurfaces is prescribed and satisfies  

(1) (Pairwise transversality, no triple points)  
    If $i\neq j$ the intersection $\Sigma_{i}\cap\Sigma_{j}$ (possibly empty) is a smooth closed $(n-2)$-submanifold, and no point of $M$ lies on three different hypersurfaces.  

(2) (Separating property)  
    For every $i$ the complement $M\setminus\Sigma_{i}$ has **exactly** two connected components.  
    One of them is declared the positive side of $\Sigma_{i}$, the other its negative side.  

Because of (1)-(2) the union $\Sigma:=\bigcup_{i}\Sigma_{i}$ decomposes $M$ into connected components, called chambers.  
Two distinct chambers are said to be adjacent when their common boundary contains an open $(n-1)$-dimensional subset lying on **exactly one** hypersurface $\Sigma_{k}$.

For every $i$ let $\operatorname{PD}_{\mathbb Z_{2}}\bigl([\Sigma_{i}]\bigr)\in H^{1}(M;\mathbb Z_{2})$ be the Poincare dual of the $\mathbb Z_{2}$-homology class $[\Sigma_{i}]$.  
Because of local finiteness the sum  
\[
\varphi\;:=\;\sum_{i=1}^{\infty} \operatorname{PD}_{\mathbb Z_{2}}\bigl([\Sigma_{i}]\bigr)\;\in\;H^{1}(M;\mathbb Z_{2})
\tag{$\ast$}
\]
is well defined.

(a)  Prove that the chambers admit a $2$-colouring (black/white) in which every two adjacent chambers receive opposite colours **iff** the cohomology class $\varphi$ defined in $(\ast)$ is zero.

(b)  Show that for $M=S^{n}$ such a colouring is always possible, no matter what countable, locally-finite family $\mathcal H$ satisfying (1)-(2) is given.
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout all (co)homology groups carry $\mathbb Z_{2}$-coefficients, so `$=$' means `$=$ mod $2$'.  Write  
\[
\Sigma=\bigcup_{i}\Sigma_{i},\qquad M^{\circ}=M\setminus\Sigma .
\]
Local finiteness implies: for every compact set $K\subset M$ there are only finitely many indices $i$ with $\Sigma_{i}\cap K\neq\varnothing$.  Consequently the infinite sum $(\ast)$ is a bona-fide $1$-cochain, hence a class in $H^{1}(M;\mathbb Z_{2})$.

Step 0.  The chamber graph.  
Choose one interior point for every chamber; these points are the vertices of a graph $\Gamma$.  
For every open $(n-1)$-piece $U\subset\Sigma_{k}$ that separates two chambers $C,C'$ pick a small segment transverse to $\Sigma_{k}$, with interior disjoint from $\Sigma$, joining the chosen points of $C$ and $C'$.  The segment represents an (unoriented) edge of $\Gamma$.  
Because $M$ is connected, any two chamber points can be joined by a smooth path that meets $\Sigma$ transversely in finitely many points; reading the chambers in the order met along the path shows that $\Gamma$ is connected.  (The separating hypothesis is essential here.)

Step 1.  Necessity of $\varphi=0$.  
Assume a $2$-colouring of the chambers is given and encode it as a $0$-cochain  
\[
f\in C^{0}(\Gamma;\mathbb Z_{2}),\qquad f(C)=
\begin{cases}
0 &\text{if $C$ is white},\\
1 &\text{if $C$ is black}.
\end{cases}
\]
Because adjacent chambers have opposite colours, the coboundary $\delta f$ evaluates to $1$ on every oriented edge of $\Gamma$.  

Fix an index $k$.  Define a $1$-cochain $e_{k}$ on $\Gamma$ by  
\[
e_{k}(E)=
\begin{cases}
1 &\text{if the edge $E$ crosses }\Sigma_{k},\\[2mm]
0 &\text{otherwise}.
\end{cases}
\]
Since every $\Sigma_{k}$ is separating, every connected component of  
$\Sigma_{k}\setminus\bigl(\bigcup_{j\neq k}\Sigma_{j}\bigr)$ appears as the common boundary
of **exactly** two adjacent chambers, hence is met by some edge of $\Gamma$; therefore $e_{k}\not\equiv 0$.  By construction  
\[
\delta f=\sum_{k}e_{k}.
\tag{1}
\]

Claim.  Each $e_{k}$ represents $\operatorname{PD}_{\mathbb Z_{2}}\bigl([\Sigma_{k}]\bigr)\in H^{1}(M;\mathbb Z_{2})$.

Indeed, let $\gamma\colon S^{1}\to M$ be a smooth closed curve transverse to $\Sigma_{k}$ and disjoint from the other hypersurfaces.  Homotope $\gamma$ so that it meets $\Gamma$ only in the mid-points of edges and meets each such edge once, transversely.  
Because $\Sigma_{k}$ and the other hypersurfaces never coincide and triple points are excluded, $\gamma$ crosses $\Sigma_{k}$ precisely once for every edge of $\Gamma$ on which $e_{k}$ takes the value $1$.  Hence the mod-$2$ intersection number of $\gamma$ with $\Sigma_{k}$ equals $e_{k}(\gamma)$, showing that $e_{k}$ is a cocycle representing the desired Poincare dual class.

Taking cohomology classes of (1) gives  
\[
0=[\delta f]=\sum_{k}\operatorname{PD}_{\mathbb Z_{2}}\bigl([\Sigma_{k}]\bigr)=\varphi ,
\]
so $\varphi=0$ is necessary.

Step 2.  Sufficiency of $\varphi=0$.  
Suppose $\varphi=0$.  Choose orientations on the edges of $\Gamma$ once and for all.  Because $\Gamma$ is a connected $1$-dimensional CW-complex,  
\[
H^{1}(\Gamma;\mathbb Z_{2})\;\cong\; \operatorname{Hom}(H_{1}(\Gamma),\mathbb Z_{2}).
\]
Under the inclusion $\iota\colon\Gamma\hookrightarrow M$ every edge crosses a single hypersurface transversely and no other, hence  
\[
\iota^{*}\Bigl(\operatorname{PD}_{\mathbb Z_{2}}\bigl([\Sigma_{k}]\bigr)\Bigr)=e_{k}.
\]
Consequently 
\[
\iota^{*}(\varphi)=\sum_{k}e_{k}\in H^{1}(\Gamma;\mathbb Z_{2}).
\]
Because $\varphi=0$ in $H^{1}(M;\mathbb Z_{2})$, its pull-back to $\Gamma$ is also zero, so the cocycle $\sum_{k}e_{k}$ is a coboundary.  Hence there exists a $0$-cochain $g\in C^{0}(\Gamma;\mathbb Z_{2})$ with  
\[
\delta g=\sum_{k}e_{k}.
\tag{2}
\]
Fix a base chamber $C_{0}$ and add the normalising condition $g(C_{0})=0$.  
Define the colour of a chamber $C$ by  
\[
\chi(C):=g(C)\in\{0,1\}.
\]
Because $\Gamma$ is connected, $g$ is uniquely determined by (2) and the normalisation, so $\chi$ is well defined.  

Let $C,C'$ be adjacent chambers joined by an oriented edge $E$ crossing $\Sigma_{k}$.  
Evaluating (2) on $E$ yields  
\[
\chi(C')-\chi(C)=\delta g(E)=\sum_{j}e_{j}(E)=e_{k}(E)=1 ,
\]
so $C$ and $C'$ receive opposite colours.  Thus $\chi$ is the sought $2$-colouring, completing the proof of (a).

Step 3.  The sphere.  
When $M=S^{n}$ we have $H^{1}(S^{n};\mathbb Z_{2})=0$, hence $\varphi=0$ for every locally-finite family $\mathcal H$.  Therefore a $2$-colouring always exists by part (a), proving (b).

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
• Topological setting upgraded from ℝ² or S² to an arbitrary closed orientable n-manifold with n ≥ 2; the argument must now work in all dimensions.  
• Instead of Jordan curves or spherical polygons we deal with smooth embedded hypersurfaces, so differential-topological notions (transversality, two-sidedness, local finiteness) appear.  
• The key obstruction and criterion for 2-colourability is formulated in cohomological language: the sum of Poincaré duals in H¹(M;ℤ₂).  Hence algebraic topology (homology/cohomology with ℤ₂, Poincaré duality, Alexander duality implicitly for separation) is indispensable.  
• Part (a) demands a two-way implication – one must both detect an obstruction and construct a colouring when the obstruction vanishes.  The construction involves path-independence arguments based on φ=0, not merely a naïve “counting circles’’ trick.  
• Part (b) recovers the classical planar/spherical result as a corollary, but only after navigating the higher-dimensional framework.  
• The necessity proof converts a hypothetical colouring into a non-trivial 1-cocycle; the sufficiency proof builds a colouring from cohomological nullity – both directions force competitors to manipulate cochains, integrals along loops, and intersection numbers mod 2.  

These layers of differential topology and algebraic topology, absent from the original and current kernels, make the enhanced variant significantly more technical and conceptually demanding.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let M^n be a closed, connected, orientable, smooth n-manifold (n \geq  2).
A countable, locally-finite family  

  H = {\Sigma _1, \Sigma _2, \ldots  }  

of pairwise distinct, connected, two-sided, smoothly embedded closed hypersurfaces is given and satisfies  

(1) Pairwise transversality. Whenever i \neq  j the intersection \Sigma _i\cap \Sigma _j (possibly empty) is a smooth closed (n-2)-submanifold, and no point of M lies on three different hypersurfaces.  

(2) Sidedness choice. For every \Sigma _i one of the two connected components of M\\Sigma _i is declared its positive side, the other its negative side.

The union \bigcup  \Sigma _i decomposes M into connected components, called chambers.
Two different chambers are adjacent if their intersection with some \Sigma _i contains an (n-1)-dimensional open set that is contained in no other \Sigma _j.

(a) Prove that the chambers admit a 2-colouring (black/white) in which every two adjacent chambers receive opposite colours if and only if  

  \varphi  := \Sigma _i PD_{\mathbb{Z}_2}([\Sigma _i]) \in  H^1(M; \mathbb{Z}_2)  

is the zero class, where PD denotes the Poincare dual with \mathbb{Z}_2-coefficients.

(b) Deduce that for M = S^n such a colouring is always possible, independently of the locally-finite family H verifying (1)-(2).
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout, all (co)homology groups carry \mathbb{Z}_2-coefficients; equality means equality mod 2.

Preliminaries  
* Because every \Sigma _i is two-sided we may speak of crossing it from its negative to its positive side, but the corrected argument will only use the total number of crossings, disregarding this orientation.  
* A generic path is a smooth path transverse to every \Sigma _i and disjoint from all pairwise intersections \Sigma _i\cap \Sigma _j.

Step 1. Colouring \Rightarrow  \varphi  = 0 (necessity)  
Assume a black/white colouring of the chambers exists.  
Choose a base chamber C_0 and declare its colour to be 0 (white); colour 1 denotes black.

Extend the colouring to a locally constant map
  f : M\\bigcup  \Sigma _i \to  \mathbb{Z}_2, f\equiv 0 on C_0,
and define the 1-cochain df by the usual difference along paths.  
Let \gamma  be any generic loop based at x_0\in C_0; each time \gamma  crosses some \Sigma _i the value of f flips, so

  \int _\gamma  df = (number of intersections of \gamma  with \bigcup \Sigma _i) = \Sigma _i \langle [\Sigma _i],[\gamma ]\rangle .

Thus the cochain df equals \Sigma _i PD([\Sigma _i]); but df is a coboundary, hence its cohomology class is zero. Therefore \varphi =0.

Step 2. \varphi =0 \Rightarrow  existence of a well-defined parity function \chi  on chambers (sufficiency)  
Assume \varphi =0.  
Fix a base chamber C_0 and set \chi (C_0)=0.

For any other chamber C choose a generic path
  \gamma : [0,1]\to M
with \gamma (0)\in C_0, \gamma (1)\in C, and define

  \chi (C) := (# of intersection points of \gamma  with \bigcup  \Sigma _i) (mod 2). (*)

Why (*) is well-defined.  
Let \gamma _1, \gamma _2 be two such paths joining C_0 to C.  Consider the closed loop
  \ell  := \gamma _1\cdot \gamma _2,
obtained by traversing \gamma _1 and returning along \gamma _2.  The parity difference produced by \gamma _1 and \gamma _2 equals the number of intersections of \ell  with \bigcup \Sigma _i, i.e.

  \chi _{\gamma _1}(C) - \chi _{\gamma _2}(C) = \Sigma _i \langle [\Sigma _i],[\ell ]\rangle  = \langle \varphi ,[\ell ]\rangle  = 0,

because \varphi =0 by hypothesis.  Hence \chi (C) does not depend on the chosen path \gamma .

Step 3. Adjacent chambers receive opposite colours  
Let C and C' be adjacent across an (n-1)-dimensional open set U\subset \Sigma _k.  
A sufficiently small path joining interior points of C and C' crosses \Sigma _k exactly once and meets no other \Sigma _i; consequently \chi (C')=\chi (C)+1 (mod 2), so the colours differ.

Step 4. Every chamber is coloured  
Local finiteness guarantees that from C_0 one can reach any chamber via a generic path meeting only finitely many \Sigma _i, so \chi  is defined on all chambers; steps 2 and 3 show that \chi  furnishes the required 2-colouring.  This completes the proof of the equivalence in (a).

Step 5. The special case M = S^n  
For the n-sphere H^1(S^n;\mathbb{Z}_2)=0, hence \varphi =0 for any family H satisfying the hypotheses.  Therefore a 2-colouring always exists, establishing part (b).

• Topological setting upgraded from ℝ² or S² to an arbitrary closed orientable n-manifold with n ≥ 2; the argument must now work in all dimensions.  
• Instead of Jordan curves or spherical polygons we deal with smooth embedded hypersurfaces, so differential-topological notions (transversality, two-sidedness, local finiteness) appear.  
• The key obstruction and criterion for 2-colourability is formulated in cohomological language: the sum of Poincaré duals in H¹(M;ℤ₂).  Hence algebraic topology (homology/cohomology with ℤ₂, Poincaré duality, Alexander duality implicitly for separation) is indispensable.  
• Part (a) demands a two-way implication – one must both detect an obstruction and construct a colouring when the obstruction vanishes.  The construction involves path-independence arguments based on φ=0, not merely a naïve “counting circles’’ trick.  
• Part (b) recovers the classical planar/spherical result as a corollary, but only after navigating the higher-dimensional framework.  
• The necessity proof converts a hypothetical colouring into a non-trivial 1-cocycle; the sufficiency proof builds a colouring from cohomological nullity – both directions force competitors to manipulate cochains, integrals along loops, and intersection numbers mod 2.  

These layers of differential topology and algebraic topology, absent from the original and current kernels, make the enhanced variant significantly more technical and conceptually demanding.
